learning_objectives: Set up a Python development environment
Write and execute basic Python programs
Understand programming fundamentals: variables, data types, operators

lecture_notes.introduction: Programming is an essential skill for health informatics professionals. Python is the language of choice for healthcare data analysis, automation, and integration. This course teaches programming through healthcare-relevant examples.
key_concepts: Why Python for health informatics: readability, data science libraries, FHIR libraries, community
Development environment: Anaconda, Jupyter notebooks, VS Code
Variables and data types: strings, integers, floats, booleans
Basic operators: arithmetic, comparison, logical
Input/output: print(), input(), string formatting
lecture_notes.demonstration: Write a simple program that calculates BMI from height and weight inputs, formatting the output with clinical interpretation

review.value: High-level programming language known for readable syntax and extensive libraries for data analysis (pandas), statistics (scipy), and healthcare (fhirclient)
Interactive coding environment mixing code, output, and documentation in one document - ideal for data analysis and exploration

learning_objectives: Write conditional statements for clinical decision logic
Implement loops for processing collections of health data
Define and call functions for reusable code

lecture_notes.introduction: Control flow and functions are the building blocks of useful programs. We learn them through healthcare examples: clinical alert logic, patient list processing, and reusable health calculations.
key_concepts: Conditional statements: if/elif/else for clinical decision logic
For loops: iterating over patient lists and data collections
While loops: processing until a condition is met
Functions: defining reusable code blocks with parameters and return values
Scope: local vs. global variables
lecture_notes.demonstration: Write a function that evaluates blood pressure readings and returns a clinical classification (normal, elevated, stage 1 HTN, stage 2 HTN, hypertensive crisis) based on ACC/AHA guidelines

review.value: if/elif/else statements that evaluate conditions and branch execution - the foundation of clinical decision support logic
Reusable code blocks that accept inputs (parameters), perform operations, and return outputs - essential for organizing healthcare data processing

learning_objectives: Use lists, dictionaries, and tuples to represent health data
Manipulate data structures to organize patient information
Understand when to use each data structure for healthcare scenarios

lecture_notes.introduction: Healthcare data naturally fits into Python data structures. Patient records are dictionaries, lab result series are lists, and vital sign measurements are tuples. Choosing the right structure makes your code clearer and faster.
key_concepts: Lists: ordered collections for lab results, medication lists, encounter history
Dictionaries: key-value pairs for patient records, code lookups, configuration
Tuples: immutable ordered collections for vital sign snapshots
List comprehensions: concise filtering and transformation of health data
Nested data structures: representing complex clinical records
lecture_notes.demonstration: Build a patient record as a nested dictionary containing demographics, medications (list), allergies (list), and vitals (list of dictionaries), then write functions to search and filter the data

review.value: Key-value data structure mapping labels to values: {'mrn': '12345', 'name': 'Jane Doe', 'dx': ['E11.9', 'I10']} - natural fit for patient records
Concise syntax for filtering/transforming lists: [p for p in patients if p['age'] > 65] - readable and efficient for health data filtering

learning_objectives: Read and write CSV files containing health data
Parse JSON data (FHIR resources) using Python
Handle file errors and data validation
readings: HL7 FHIR Patient Resource JSON Example

lecture_notes.introduction: Health informatics professionals constantly work with data files. CSV files for reports and bulk data, JSON for FHIR APIs, and XML for C-CDA documents. Python makes reading and manipulating these files straightforward.
key_concepts: CSV reading/writing with the csv module and pandas
JSON parsing: loading FHIR resources, navigating nested structures
File handling: open, read, write, close, context managers (with statement)
Error handling: try/except for robust data processing
Data validation: checking types, ranges, and required fields in health data
lecture_notes.demonstration: Read a CSV file of lab results, parse a FHIR Patient resource from JSON, and combine data into a summary report

review.value: json.loads() converts JSON string to Python dictionary - essential for working with FHIR API responses
try/except blocks catch and handle errors gracefully - critical when processing health data where missing values and format inconsistencies are common

class_schedule.topic: Introduction to pandas for Healthcare Data Analysis
learning_objectives: Use pandas DataFrames to organize and analyze health datasets
Perform filtering, grouping, and aggregation on clinical data
Create basic data visualizations from health data

lecture_notes.introduction: pandas is the most important Python library for health data analysis. It provides DataFrame objects that make working with tabular health data intuitive and powerful.
key_concepts: DataFrame creation from CSV, JSON, and dictionaries
Selecting and filtering: column selection, boolean indexing, .loc/.iloc
Grouping and aggregation: groupby() for calculating metrics by diagnosis, provider, unit
Missing data handling: isna(), fillna(), dropna() for health data quality
Basic visualization: matplotlib/seaborn plots from DataFrames
lecture_notes.demonstration: Load a hospital discharge dataset, calculate average length of stay by diagnosis, find readmission rates, and visualize results
setup_requirements: Jupyter notebook with pandas, matplotlib, seaborn

review.value: Two-dimensional tabular data structure with labeled rows and columns - think of it as a programmable spreadsheet optimized for data analysis
Split-apply-combine operation: group data by a column (e.g., diagnosis), apply a function (mean, count), combine results - essential for healthcare metrics

class_schedule.topic: APIs and FHIR for Health Data Access
learning_objectives: Make HTTP requests to healthcare APIs using Python
Query a FHIR server for patient data programmatically
Process and analyze API responses in Python
readings: FHIR RESTful API Quick Start Guide

lecture_notes.introduction: Modern health data access is API-driven. FHIR APIs are becoming the standard for accessing patient data from EHRs. Learning to interact with these APIs programmatically is a critical health informatics skill.
key_concepts: HTTP fundamentals: GET, POST, headers, status codes, authentication
Python requests library for API calls
FHIR RESTful operations: read, search, create using Python
Parsing FHIR Bundle resources and extracting clinical data
Building a simple health data pipeline: API call, parse, analyze, visualize
lecture_notes.demonstration: Connect to a FHIR test server, search for diabetic patients, retrieve their lab results, and calculate A1c trends using pandas
setup_requirements: FHIR test server (HAPI FHIR public sandbox)

review.value: GET /Patient?name=Smith or GET /Observation?code=4548-4 (A1c) - standard URL patterns for querying FHIR servers
Container resource returned by FHIR search containing multiple entries - iterate through bundle['entry'] to process each result

questions.question: Write a Python function called classify_a1c that takes an A1c value (float) as input and returns a string classification: 'Normal' for A1c < 5.7, 'Pre-diabetes' for 5.7 <= A1c < 6.5, 'Diabetes' for A1c >= 6.5. Include input validation that raises a ValueError if the input is negative.

rubric.key: Clean code style and return values

questions.question: Given a list of patient dictionaries, write code to find all patients over age 65 with a diagnosis of hypertension (ICD-10 code starting with 'I10') and return their names and ages as a list of tuples.
questions.answer:
patients = [
    {'name': 'John Smith', 'age': 72, 'diagnoses': ['I10', 'E11.9']},
    {'name': 'Jane Doe', 'age': 45, 'diagnoses': ['J45.20']},
    {'name': 'Bob Jones', 'age': 68, 'diagnoses': ['I10', 'I25.10']}
]

result = [(p['name'], p['age']) for p in patients if p['age'] > 65 and any(dx.startswith('I10') for dx in p['diagnoses'])]
# result: [('John Smith', 72), ('Bob Jones', 68)]

rubric.key: Correct diagnosis code matching with startswith
Proper use of list comprehension or loop

questions.question: Write Python code using pandas to read a CSV file called 'discharges.csv', calculate the average length of stay (LOS) grouped by the 'primary_diagnosis' column, and display the top 5 diagnoses with the highest average LOS.
questions.answer:
import pandas as pd

df = pd.read_csv('discharges.csv')
avg_los = df.groupby('primary_diagnosis')['los'].mean()
top5 = avg_los.sort_values(ascending=False).head(5)
print(top5)

questions.question: Write Python code to parse the following FHIR Patient JSON and extract the patient's full name, birth date, and MRN (from the identifier array where system contains 'mrn'). Handle the case where any field might be missing.
questions.answer:
import json

patient_json = '{"resourceType": "Patient", "name": [{"family": "Smith", "given": ["John"]}], "birthDate": "1955-03-15", "identifier": [{"system": "http://hospital.org/mrn", "value": "MRN12345"}]}'

patient = json.loads(patient_json)

try:
    name = patient['name'][0]
    full_name = f"{name.get('given', [''])[0]} {name.get('family', '')}"
except (KeyError, IndexError):
    full_name = 'Unknown'

birth_date = patient.get('birthDate', 'Unknown')

mrn = 'Unknown'
for identifier in patient.get('identifier', []):
    if 'mrn' in identifier.get('system', '').lower():
        mrn = identifier.get('value', 'Unknown')
        break

print(f'Name: {full_name}, DOB: {birth_date}, MRN: {mrn}')

rubric.key: Proper name extraction from FHIR name array

major_assignments.title: Healthcare Data Analysis Pipeline
major_assignments.description: Build a Python data analysis pipeline that reads healthcare data from a CSV file, cleans and validates it, performs meaningful clinical analysis, and produces a formatted report with visualizations.
objectives: Apply Python programming skills to a realistic health data analysis task
Implement data validation and cleaning for clinical data
Use pandas for healthcare data analysis and aggregation
Create meaningful visualizations of health data
deliverables: Jupyter notebook with complete analysis pipeline
At least 5 meaningful clinical analyses with pandas
Written interpretation of findings (in notebook markdown cells)

criteria: Proper use of functions for reusable logic
Good variable naming and code organization
Correct use of pandas groupby, filtering, and aggregation
Clean, well-organized Python code with proper error handling
Thorough data validation and quality assessment

weekly_quizzes.description: Assessment of programming concepts and Python skills

lab_assignments.description: Weekly coding exercises applying programming to health informatics scenarios

midterm_examination.description: Comprehensive assessment of Python fundamentals and data structures

final_examination.description: Cumulative exam including pandas, file processing, and API interaction

course_policies.attendance: Lab sessions are mandatory. Programming is a skill that requires practice. More than 2 unexcused lab absences result in grade reduction.
course_policies.late_submissions: Late code submissions lose 10% per day. All code must run without errors to receive credit.
course_policies.collaboration: Discussion of programming concepts is encouraged. All code must be individually written. Identical code submissions are treated as academic dishonesty.
course_policies.academic_integrity: All code must be the student's own work. Use of AI coding assistants must be disclosed and the student must explain every line. Copying code without attribution is plagiarism.
course_policies.technology_requirements: Laptop with Anaconda Python, Jupyter notebooks, and VS Code. All software is free and cross-platform.
